common_name: Stella d'Oro Daylily (Gold Stella Miniature Daylily)

planting.note: Plant bare-root fans or container divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to light partial shade; Stella d'Oro establishes quickly and begins blooming within weeks of planting, performing best with 6+ hours of sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Stella d'Oro is the most widely planted daylily cultivar in American horticulture — a compact miniature reaching just 12–14 inches tall with golden-yellow, slightly fragrant, 2.75-inch flowers produced continuously and prolifically from late spring through hard frost. It won the Stout Silver Medal in 1985 and was the best-selling perennial in the United States for many years. The near-continuous bloom production sets it apart from many daylilies that bloom for only 2–3 weeks; Stella d'Oro has an extended rebloom cycle that fills the garden with color all season. Deadhead spent flower stalks at the base to maintain a tidy appearance and encourage additional bloom. Divide every 3–4 years when the clump becomes crowded and bloom quality decreases. Excellent for zone 9 where it blooms from March through December.
sources: American Hemerocallis Society — Stout Silver Medal

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to light partial shade; Happy Returns is a sister seedling to Stella d'Oro and shares its outstanding rebloom habit, compact size, and adaptability.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Happy Returns is a classic reblooming compact daylily producing slightly fragrant, soft lemon-yellow, ruffled 3-inch flowers continuously from early summer through fall. Bred as a Stella d'Oro companion with cooler, lighter yellow coloring that blends more subtly into the mixed border, it blooms at the same time and repeat-blooms reliably through the season. The soft lemon color complements blues, purples, and whites beautifully in perennial borders. Happy Returns is notably fragrant at dusk, a quality unusual in modern daylilies. Divide every 3–4 years when the center of the clump becomes woody and bloom declines. In zone 9 it begins blooming in April and continues through November with semi-evergreen foliage through mild Gulf Coast winters.

common_name: Pardon Me Daylily (Red Miniature Reblooming Daylily)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Pardon Me is a compact reblooming red daylily excellent for containers, edging, and mass plantings in zone 3–10.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Pardon Me is the premier compact red reblooming daylily — producing velvety, deep cranberry-red flowers with a small yellow-green throat on compact 18–22 inch stems from early summer through fall with reliable repeat bloom throughout the season. It won the Stout Silver Medal in 1994 and remains one of the most consistent compact red daylilies available. The deep red color holds through summer heat without significant fading, an important quality in red-pigmented daylilies that can bleach in direct sun. Pardon Me is a fragrant cultivar with a pleasant fruity-floral scent detectable especially in morning and evening. In zone 9 it begins blooming in April and continues through November in good conditions. Divide every 3–4 years when bloom declines.
sources: American Hemerocallis Society — Stout Silver Medal

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to light partial shade; Hyperion performs best with 6+ hours of full sun and regular fertilization through the growing season.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Hyperion is one of the oldest and most beloved daylily cultivars still in wide cultivation — introduced in 1924, it remains a benchmark for fragrance, elegance, and reliability nearly a century later. The tall flower scapes reach 3–4 feet and carry large (4.5-inch), clear canary-yellow, slightly ruffled flowers with an outstanding, intensely sweet, lily-like fragrance that perfumes the garden on warm evenings. It blooms in midsummer for approximately 3–4 weeks and does not rebloom reliably, but the elegance and fragrance of its single season have kept it popular for a century. The tall stems are excellent for cutting and make spectacular vase arrangements. Though long surpassed technically by modern hybrids, its combination of fragrance, classic proportions, and reliable performance makes it a worthwhile choice for the perennial garden. Divide every 4–5 years.

common_name: Rosy Returns Daylily (Pink Reblooming Miniature Daylily)

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Rosy Returns reblooms most vigorously when kept consistently moist between bloom cycles.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Rosy Returns is a compact prolific-reblooming daylily producing rose-pink flowers with a yellow-green throat and darker rose eye zone from early summer through fall in multiple reliable flushes. It was bred as a Stella d'Oro companion that extends the rebloom series into pink tones while maintaining the same outstanding rebloom habit. The soft rose-pink coloring is attractive and distinctive among reblooming daylilies, which tend to be dominated by yellow and orange. Rosy Returns combines beautifully with white, purple, or blue perennial companions in the long-season border. In zone 9 it begins blooming in March or April and typically continues through November, often with semi-evergreen foliage through mild Gulf Coast winters. Divide every 3–4 years.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Chicago Apache requires full sun to develop and hold its intense scarlet-red coloring — in shade or partial shade the flower color is significantly less vivid and bloom production decreases.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Chicago Apache is widely regarded as one of the finest scarlet-red daylilies — a mid-season bloomer producing large (5-inch), velvety, brilliant scarlet-red flowers with a small yellow throat on 26–28 inch scapes. The intense glowing red color holds through the heat of summer without significant fading, a difficulty with many red and orange daylilies. It was introduced by James Marsh of the famous 'Chicago' hybridizing program and was awarded the Stout Silver Medal in 1986. The large, well-formed flowers make it an outstanding specimen plant or mass-planting accent in the full-sun border. Though it does not rebloom as prolifically as miniature varieties, it produces abundant scapes over a 3–4 week main bloom season. The red color is striking when planted alongside white or pale-yellow companions. Divide every 3–4 years when center growth becomes congested.
sources: American Hemerocallis Society — Stout Silver Medal
